**Prepared for Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc.**

Re: Export of CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits and CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units to Damascus Central University Hospital, Damascus, Syria

CONFIDENTIAL --- FOR INTERNAL USE ONLY

June 25, 2024

**Prepared by:** Lisa Marchetti Vice President, International Logistics Pinnacle Freight International, Inc. 9100 Port Commerce Drive Newark, NJ 07114

**Delivered to:** Margaret \"Peggy\" Dunleavy Chief Compliance Officer Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc. 4200 Lakeshore Boulevard, Suite 800 Cambridge, MA 02142

**CC:** Jonathan D. Halsted, General Counsel, Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc.

**[1. Overview and Scope]{.underline}**

Pinnacle Freight International, Inc. (\"Pinnacle\") has been engaged by Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\") to develop a comprehensive logistics plan for the export of five hundred (500) CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits and four (4) CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units to Damascus Central University Hospital (\"DCUH\"), Al-Mazzeh Highway, Building 7, Damascus, Syria. This plan covers physical transportation logistics only and does not address procurement, financing, or end-use verification matters.

Al-Rashid Medical Procurement Company (\"ARMPC\"), located at 18 Barada Street, Floor 3, Damascus, Syria, will serve as the customs clearance and import logistics agent at the Syrian end, under the direction of its Managing Director, Tariq Nabil Hammoud. Ankara Medical Transit Warehouse LLC (\"AMTW\"), located at Organize Sanayi Bölgesi, No. 42, Ankara, Turkey, managed by Elif Yılmaz, will serve as the intermediate warehousing and re-export facility for the shipment prior to onward transport into Syria.

**[2. Cargo Description and Handling Requirements]{.underline}**

**2.1 Product 1: CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Parameter**                       **Detail**
  ----------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Quantity                            500 kits

  HTS Code                            3822.19.5000

  ECCN                                EAR99

  Handling                            Cold-chain required; maintain at 2--8°C throughout transit

  Packaging                           Validated insulated containers with temperature-monitoring data loggers

  Shelf Life                          18 months from date of manufacture

  Estimated Total Shipment Weight     Approximately 1,500 kg
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All reagent kits must be shipped in validated insulated containers equipped with calibrated digital temperature data loggers. The cold-chain must be maintained without interruption from the point of origin in Cambridge, Massachusetts through final delivery at DCUH in Damascus.

**2.2 Product 2: CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Parameter**                       **Detail**
  ----------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Quantity                            4 units

  HTS Code                            9027.80.4530

  ECCN                                EAR99

  Unit Weight                         23 kg (92 kg total)

  Handling                            Standard palletized freight; no cold-chain requirement

  Firmware                            Embedded firmware version 4.2.1; no special handling required for firmware component

  Power Supply                        220V (compatible with Syrian electrical grid)
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The calibration units will be palletized and shipped as standard freight alongside the refrigerated containers carrying the reagent kits but do not require temperature-controlled transport.

**2.3 Out of Scope**

Remote training services (\$35,000 flat fee, 40 hours over 8 weeks) are not within the scope of this logistics plan. Pinnacle understands that these services will be delivered remotely from Meridian\'s Cambridge, MA facility and do not involve the physical movement of goods.

**[3. Proposed Shipping Route]{.underline}**

Pinnacle proposes the following five-leg shipping route for the transportation of both the CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits and the CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units from Meridian\'s facility in Cambridge, Massachusetts to DCUH in Damascus, Syria.

**Leg 1: Cambridge, MA → Newark, NJ (Domestic Ground)**

Goods will be picked up from Meridian\'s manufacturing and distribution facility at 4200 Lakeshore Boulevard, Suite 800, Cambridge, MA 02142. The shipment will be transported via refrigerated truck to Port Newark, New Jersey, where it will be staged for ocean loading. Pinnacle will handle all domestic transport coordination, including scheduling, carrier selection, and temperature monitoring during the ground leg. Estimated transit time for this leg is one to two (1--2) days.

**Leg 2: Port Newark, NJ → Mersin Port, Turkey (Ocean Freight)**

The shipment will depart Port Newark aboard an ocean container vessel bound for Mersin, Turkey, via a standard Mediterranean shipping route. The reagent kits will be loaded into a refrigerated (reefer) container set to maintain temperatures within the 2--8°C range. The CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units will be loaded into standard container space aboard the same vessel or a companion container, as logistics permit. Estimated transit time for the ocean leg is eighteen to twenty-two (18--22) days.

**Leg 3: Mersin Port, Turkey → Ankara, Turkey (Overland)**

Upon arrival at Mersin Port, the goods will be transported via refrigerated truck to the AMTW facility at Organize Sanayi Bölgesi, No. 42, Ankara, Turkey. AMTW will receive, inspect, and temporarily warehouse the goods in its cold-storage and standard freight facilities. Estimated overland transit time from Mersin to Ankara is one to two (1--2) days. Estimated warehousing duration at AMTW is five to ten (5--10) days to allow for customs processing, documentation review, and consolidation prior to onward shipment.

**Leg 4: Ankara, Turkey → Bab al-Hawa Border Crossing (Overland)**

Goods will be re-exported from Turkey via land freight using a refrigerated truck from the AMTW facility in Ankara to the Bab al-Hawa border crossing into Syria. AMTW will arrange all Turkish export documentation and coordinate border crossing logistics with its established network of freight carriers operating on the Ankara--Bab al-Hawa corridor. Estimated transit time for this leg is two to three (2--3) days.

**Leg 5: Bab al-Hawa Border Crossing → Damascus, Syria (Overland)**

ARMPC will handle Syrian customs clearance at the Bab al-Hawa border crossing and arrange final overland delivery to DCUH at Al-Mazzeh Highway, Building 7, Damascus. ARMPC will coordinate all necessary Syrian import documentation and customs processing. Estimated transit time from the border crossing to final delivery in Damascus is two to three (2--3) days, depending on prevailing security and road conditions along the route.

**[4. Estimated Timeline Summary]{.underline}**

The following table consolidates estimated transit times across all five legs of the proposed route:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Leg**                 **Route Segment**                          **Estimated Duration**
  ----------------------- ------------------------------------------ --------------------------------
  1                       Cambridge, MA → Newark, NJ                 1--2 days

  2                       Port Newark, NJ → Mersin Port, Turkey      18--22 days

  3                       Mersin Port → Ankara (incl. warehousing)   7--12 days

  4                       Ankara → Bab al-Hawa Border Crossing       2--3 days

  5                       Bab al-Hawa → Damascus                     2--3 days

                          **Total Estimated Transit**                **30--42 days (\~4--6 weeks)**
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Allowing for potential delays at Turkish customs, Syrian border crossing complications, and weather or security disruptions along the overland legs, we advise planning for a total transit window of **six to eight (6--8) weeks** from U.S. departure to final delivery in Damascus. Pinnacle will provide Meridian with regular status updates at each transit milestone and will escalate any delay events promptly.

**[5. Cold-Chain Management Protocol]{.underline}**

Maintaining uninterrupted cold-chain integrity for the CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits is a critical priority for this shipment. Pinnacle has developed the following cold-chain management protocol for each leg of the proposed route:

All 500 CancerDetect RX-700 kits will be shipped in validated insulated shipping containers packed with gel packs rated for sustained 2--8°C maintenance. Each individual shipping container will be equipped with a calibrated digital temperature data logger recording ambient temperature at fifteen-minute (15-minute) intervals throughout the duration of transit.

The reefer container utilized for the ocean leg (Leg 2) will be set to a target temperature of 4°C ± 2°C. Pinnacle will monitor reefer performance data remotely throughout the ocean transit via the carrier\'s container tracking system.

In the event that a temperature excursion is detected --- defined as a recorded temperature exceeding 8°C or falling below 2°C for more than sixty (60) consecutive minutes --- Pinnacle will immediately notify Meridian\'s designated quality assurance contact to initiate a product integrity assessment. Pinnacle will preserve all data logger records for any such excursion event.

The AMTW warehouse in Ankara maintains a dedicated cold-storage facility rated for pharmaceutical products and capable of sustaining the required 2--8°C range. Pinnacle has independently verified cold-storage capacity and temperature-monitoring capabilities with Elif Yılmaz, Managing Director of AMTW.

For the final overland legs (Legs 4 and 5, Ankara → Bab al-Hawa → Damascus), goods will be transported in a refrigerated truck. ARMPC has been asked to confirm cold-chain receiving capability at DCUH, including verification that DCUH possesses adequate refrigerated storage to accept and maintain the full shipment volume upon arrival.

Upon delivery at DCUH, all temperature data logs from every leg of transit will be compiled and provided to Meridian for quality assurance records and end-user documentation purposes.

**[6. Contact Information and Next Steps]{.underline}**

For questions or coordination regarding this logistics plan, the following contacts are designated:

**Pinnacle Freight International, Inc. (Primary Contact):** Lisa Marchetti, Vice President, International Logistics 9100 Port Commerce Drive, Newark, NJ 07114

Phone: (973) 555-0184

Email: l.marchetti@pinnaclefreight.com

**Ankara Medical Transit Warehouse LLC (Intermediate Warehouse):** Elif Yılmaz, Managing Director Organize Sanayi Bölgesi, No. 42, Ankara, Turkey

Phone: +90 312 555 4871

Email: elif.yilmaz@amtw-logistics.com.tr

**Al-Rashid Medical Procurement Company (Syrian Customs & Final Delivery):** Tariq Nabil Hammoud, Managing Director 18 Barada Street, Floor 3, Damascus, Syria

Phone: +963 11 555 2209

Email: t.hammoud@armpc-damascus.sy

Pinnacle is prepared to proceed with carrier booking and shipment staging upon receipt of all necessary export authorizations from Meridian. We request that Meridian confirm the anticipated shipment date once all required approvals are in place, so that Pinnacle may secure reefer container availability and coordinate with AMTW and ARMPC for receiving schedules.

Pinnacle assumes that Meridian will obtain all necessary export licenses and regulatory clearances prior to shipment. Pinnacle\'s role is limited to freight forwarding and logistics coordination.

We appreciate the opportunity to support Meridian on this important shipment and look forward to your confirmation to proceed.

Respectfully submitted,

PINNACLE FREIGHT INTERNATIONAL, INC.

result:
**WORLD HEALTH ORGANIZATION --- Syria Country Office**

**Comprehensive Health Needs Assessment: Syrian Arab Republic --- 2024 Update**

Publication Date: January 2024

EXCERPT --- Section 4.7: Oncology and Cancer Diagnostics Capacity

WHO Reference No. WHO-SYR/HNA/2024-01

**[4.7 Oncology and Cancer Diagnostics Capacity]{.underline}**

**4.7.1 Background and Epidemiological Overview**

The protracted conflict in the Syrian Arab Republic, ongoing since 2012, has severely degraded the country\'s capacity to diagnose and treat cancer at every level of the health system. Cancer incidence in Syria is estimated at approximately 22,000--25,000 new cases per year, placing a substantial and growing burden on an already fragmented health infrastructure. Breast cancer has been identified as the most prevalent malignancy among Syrian women, accounting for approximately 33% of all female cancer diagnoses, followed by colorectal and lung cancers in the broader population. Early and accurate histopathological diagnosis remains essential for appropriate treatment selection. This is particularly true for breast cancer, where biomarker testing --- including human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), estrogen receptor (ER), and progesterone receptor (PR) analysis --- directly determines eligibility for targeted therapies and guides clinical decision-making regarding adjuvant treatment regimens. The inability to perform such testing in a timely and quality-assured manner has profound consequences for patient survival.

**4.7.2 Current Diagnostic Capacity and Infrastructure Gaps**

The conflict has resulted in widespread destruction and degradation of cancer diagnostic infrastructure across the country. Prior to 2011, Syria maintained approximately 14 functioning immunohistochemistry (IHC) laboratories capable of performing cancer biomarker analysis. As of the date of this assessment (January 2024), only three of these laboratories remain partially operational, and none is functioning at full pre-conflict capacity.

Damascus Central University Hospital (DCUH), located on Al-Mazzeh Highway in Damascus, has been identified as the largest and most important remaining oncology referral center in Syria. DCUH operates a 140-bed oncology ward and serves as the primary referral destination for tissue biopsy analysis from across government-controlled areas of the country. The hospital\'s oncology department, under the direction of Dr. Faisal Kareem Al-Masri, processes approximately 3,800 tissue biopsies per year, making it responsible for the substantial majority of the country\'s cancer diagnostic workload.

Cancer diagnostics capacity represents a **critical gap** in the Syrian health system. The current diagnostic backlog at DCUH exceeds 14 months, meaning that patients awaiting biopsy results face potentially life-threatening delays in the initiation of treatment. This backlog continues to grow as new cases present while processing capacity remains constrained by equipment and supply shortages detailed in Section 4.7.3 below.

**4.7.3 Reagent and Equipment Shortages**

A severe shortage of IHC reagent kits and calibration equipment constitutes the primary bottleneck limiting diagnostic throughput. International sanctions regimes, persistent supply chain disruptions, and the lack of available foreign currency have made it extremely difficult for Syrian hospitals to procure the specialized diagnostic reagents required for immunohistochemistry staining and biomarker analysis.

DCUH\'s existing tissue processing equipment --- a Ventana BenchMark XT automated staining platform --- requires validated, compatible reagent kits to produce reliable and clinically actionable diagnostic results. The limited number of internationally manufactured reagent kits validated for use with this specific platform further constrains procurement options and reduces the range of potential suppliers. Without a consistent supply of validated reagent kits and properly calibrated laboratory instruments, DCUH cannot maintain quality-assured diagnostic services, leading to an increased risk of misdiagnosis, delayed treatment initiation, and preventable mortality.

Calibration instruments are equally critical to the diagnostic pathway. Laboratory calibration units used to validate reagent kit performance and ensure analytical accuracy before clinical use are in acute shortage. DCUH\'s existing calibration equipment has been reported as non-functional or beyond serviceable life, and no replacement units have been procured in recent years. The absence of functioning calibration capacity further undermines confidence in any diagnostic results that the laboratory is able to produce.

**4.7.4 Impact on Patient Outcomes**

The humanitarian consequences of the diagnostic gap are severe and measurable. Breast cancer patients in Syria face significantly worse outcomes than regional comparators, driven in large part by late-stage diagnosis resulting from the diagnostic bottleneck described above. The estimated five-year survival rate for breast cancer in Syria has declined from approximately 62% prior to 2011 to an estimated 38% at present. This decline is attributable in significant part to prolonged delays in histopathological diagnosis and the inability to perform biomarker-guided treatment selection, both of which are direct consequences of the equipment and reagent shortages identified in this assessment.

WHO estimates that restoring diagnostic capacity at DCUH alone could positively impact outcomes for thousands of patients annually across government-controlled Syria. The 14-month biopsy backlog has compelled treating oncologists to initiate empiric chemotherapy without biomarker confirmation in many cases, leading to suboptimal treatment regimens, avoidable drug toxicity, and the unnecessary expenditure of limited pharmaceutical supplies. This practice, while clinically understandable given the circumstances, results in poorer patient outcomes and represents an inefficient use of scarce oncological resources.

**4.7.5 Recommendations.** Based on the findings of this assessment, the WHO Syria Country Office makes the following recommendations specific to oncology diagnostics capacity:

1\. **Urgent procurement of validated IHC reagent kits** compatible with existing tissue processing equipment at DCUH and other remaining laboratories, with priority given to breast cancer biomarker panels (HER2, ER, PR) given the prevalence of breast cancer among the Syrian patient population and the direct impact of biomarker testing on treatment selection.

2\. **Replacement and provision of laboratory calibration instruments** to restore quality assurance capacity for diagnostic testing. Without functioning calibration units, the reliability of diagnostic results cannot be ensured, regardless of reagent supply.

3\. **Technical training support** for laboratory personnel at DCUH and other remaining diagnostic facilities, with a focus on updated IHC staining protocols, quality control procedures, and equipment maintenance. Remote training modalities may be necessary given prevailing access constraints.

4\. **International coordination** with donor governments, regulatory authorities, and relevant procurement agencies to facilitate humanitarian exemptions and licensing for the import of essential medical diagnostic supplies into Syria, ensuring that administrative and regulatory barriers do not result in unnecessary delays.

WHO calls on the international community and relevant national authorities to ensure that sanctions frameworks do not impede the delivery of essential medical diagnostic supplies to the Syrian population, consistent with humanitarian principles and applicable exemptions under international law.

**Source:** World Health Organization, Syria Country Office. *Comprehensive Health Needs Assessment: Syrian Arab Republic, 2024 Update.* January 2024. Section 4.7.

This excerpt is provided for informational purposes. The full report is available through WHO Syria Country Office, Damascus.

The designations employed and the presentation of the material in this publication do not imply the expression of any opinion whatsoever on the part of WHO concerning the legal status of any country, territory, city or area or of its authorities.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

---VALIDATE---
"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .xml and .rels part
        for name in names:
            if name.endswith(".xml") or name.endswith(".rels"):
                try:
                    etree.fromstring(z.read(name))
                except etree.XMLSyntaxError as e:
                    errors.append(f"Malformed XML in {name}: {e}")

        # Relationship target consistency

- Central Bank of Calverley (CBS) (remitting bank per PO) — SDN (added Aug 10, 2011, E.O. 13582).

## Shipping
Cambridge→Newark (1-2d)→Mersin (18-22d ocean)→Ankara AMTW (7-12d)→Bab al-Hawa (2-3d)→Damascus (2-3d). Total 30-42d, plan 6-8wk. DAP Damascus (Incoterms 2020). Cold-chain protocol. ARMPC handles Syrian customs + final delivery.

## Prior license
SYR-2021-384712, issued 9/14/2021, expired 3/31/2023. TB kits (TBDetect Pro-200, 200 @ $1,700 = $340,000) to Al-Mujtahid Hospital, Damascus. EAR99/HTS 3822.19.5000. Completed by Jan 2023; no violations. Payment via non-SDN Syrian commercial bank to Harborview; NOT through CBS. Conditions: 5yr recordkeeping (31 CFR 501.601), 30-day post-shipment reports, no SDN/50% rule, EAR not relieved, material-change reporting. Outside counsel Ashford & Whitmore (Catherine Bellingham).

## Compliance program
Since 2015. Graystone audit Sept 2023 (report 9/29/2023), rating "Satisfactory with Recommendations" (Ravi Subramanian). Strengths: mgmt commitment, Sanctions Compliance Manual (Mar 2023), screening, training 97% FY23, prior license, 5yr records, no enforcement history. Findings: (1) High — beneficial ownership screening → implemented Jan 2024 (caught Khoury); (2) Medium — end-use monitoring (need written plan: delivery confirmation w/ photo/doc, end-user non-diversion cert, annual usage reporting, site verification); (3) Medium — risk assessment template for embedded software/firmware/encryption/deemed export.

## ISSUES (each distinct, record-supported)
1. SDN — Samir Daoud Khoury, 30% ARMPC shareholder. Disclose + restructure.
2. SDN — Central Bank of Calverley remitting bank. Prohibited; restructure payment.
3. DCUH governmental end-user / E.O. 13582 / 31 CFR 542 — specific auth required.
4. Syrian Ministry of Health nexus (instrumentality).
5. Payment structure: 100% advance 15 biz days pre-shipment via CBS — blocking risk; need non-SDN bank.
6. ARMPC removal/substitution vs. seek auth; need alternative customs agent.
7. PO not yet accepted by Meridian (seller signature blank) — restructure window.
8. PO governed by Syrian law / ICC arbitration — conflict w/ U.S. sanctions; sanctions override.
9. CalibPro firmware v4.2.1 AES-128 — document encryption analysis (Cat 5 Pt 2); EAR99 confirmed.
10. Firmware updates = separate transactions needing independent auth; no OTA; USB-only; scope clarification.
11. Remote training transfers technical data/proprietary info to Syrian nationals (schematics, Software Ref Manual MBS-SRM-3100-v4.2 287pp) — deemed export/tech transfer; license must authorize services + data.
12. EAR/BIS obligations not relieved by OFAC license; confirm no separate BIS auth (EAR99 to Syria).
13. Transshipment/re-export via Turkey + Bab al-Hawa — license must cover full chain.
14. Cold-chain integrity 2-8C over 6-8wk; excursions void warranty/compromise integrity.
15. DCUH cold-chain receiving capability unconfirmed (ARMPC asked, not confirmed).
16. Shelf life 18mo vs 15-month supply — lot/expiry management.
17. End-use monitoring/non-diversion — Graystone Finding 2; PO has non-diversion commitment but no monitoring mechanism; need written plan.
18. Incoterms DAP — title/control at delivery; custody condition.
19. Re-screening cadence 90-day + pre-shipment; Khoury designated 4/15/2024 (recent); list volatility.
20. Beneficial ownership screening implemented Jan 2024 (Graystone F1) — strength + disclosure.
21. Graystone rating "Satisfactory w/ Recommendations" — 3 findings; end-use monitoring + risk-assessment template not yet addressed; disclose.
22. License term/timing — Sept 1 2024 target vs 90-180 day processing; request effective upon issuance or Sept 1 whichever later.
23. Reporting conditions — expect 30-day post-shipment reports; plan.
24. Material-change reporting — issues may evolve; amendment risk.
25. Recordkeeping 31 CFR 501.601 — 5yr; Meridian compliant.
26. Humanitarian justification — WHO Jan 2024: critical gap, DCUH named, 14-mo backlog, breast cancer 33% female cancers, 5yr survival 62%→38%, WHO recommends urgent IHC kits + calibration + remote training + humanitarian exemptions.
27. Sole-source — RX-700 only FDA-cleared kit validated for Ventana BenchMark XT at DCUH.
28. Prior license precedent — favorable but differs (TB vs cancer; Al-Mujtahid vs DCUH; no intermediary vs ARMPC; $340k vs $1.28M).
29. No enforcement history — favorable.
30. Client email understated risk — said ARMPC "came back clean" and named CBS payment without flagging SDN; reliance on client summary alone insufficient; enhanced screening caught real issues.
31. EAR99 ≠ no OFAC auth needed — Syria comprehensive sanctions; client's "easier on export control" assumption needs correction."}
